name: Campo de Calatrava Loop
name_original: Calzada de Calatrava y Santa Cruz de Mudela por Villalba de Calatrava — circular por Granatula de Calatrava
description: This moderate 56.0-mile road cycling route explores the Campo de Calatrava region, passing through historic towns and scenic landscapes.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Granátula De Calatrava traverse a landscape characterized by the plains of Campo de Calatrava, river valleys such as the Río Jabalón, and the distant hills of Sierra de Puertollano. The region offers a mix of open agricultural areas and routes connecting historical towns. Elevation changes are present, ranging from gentle undulations along riverbeds to more sustained climbs in the hiller sections. Best road cycling routes around Granátula De Calatrava The most popular road cycling route is Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro, a 53.4 miles (85.9 km) trail that takes 3 hours 25 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers a circular journey connecting several towns and rural areas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is From Aldea del Rey to Pozuelo de Calatrava — circular by the Río Jabalón, a moderate 33.8 miles (54.4 km) path. This route follows the Río Jabalón, providing views of the river and surrounding countryside. Local road cyclists also love the Bolaños, Torralba y Carrión de Calatrava — circular desde Almagro, a 27.9 miles (45.0 km) trail leading through the Campo de Calatrava, often completed in about 1 hour 43 minutes. Road cycling around Granátula De Calatrava is defined by the open plains of Campo de Calatrava, riverine landscapes along the Río Jabalón, and the rolling hills of the Sierra de Puertollano. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes to suit various ability levels. The routes in Granátula De Calatrava are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from around 18 reviews. More than 380 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Granátula De Calatrava's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Granátula De Calatrava?
A: There are 23 road cycling routes around Granátula De Calatrava, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels.

Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find in Granátula De Calatrava?
A: Granátula De Calatrava offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, including 5 easy, 13 moderate, and 5 difficult options, catering to various fitness levels and preferences.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, there are 5 easy road cycling routes around Granátula De Calatrava, perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed ride. One popular easy route is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249647502/bolanos-torralba-y-carrion-de-calatrava-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bolaños, Torralba y Carrión de Calatrava — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt;, which is 27.9 miles (45.0 km) long.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Granátula De Calatrava?
A: The road cycling routes around Granátula De Calatrava traverse a landscape characterized by the open plains of Campo de Calatrava, river valleys such as the Río Jabalón, and the distant rolling hills of Sierra de Puertollano. You'll find a mix of agricultural areas and routes connecting historical towns.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for advanced riders?
A: Yes, for advanced riders seeking a challenge, there are 5 difficult road cycling routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1246843298/sierra-de-puertollano-and-huertezuelas-de-calatrava-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Puertollano and Huertezuelas de Calatrava Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 83.2 miles (133.9 km) with significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available around Granátula De Calatrava?
A: Many routes in the region are circular, offering convenient starting and ending points. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251905687/pozuelo-de-calatrava-miguelturra-y-ermita-de-santa-marina-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251868469/campo-de-calatrava-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campo de Calatrava Loop&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year for road biking in Granátula De Calatrava?
A: While specific data isn't provided, the region's climate suggests spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures for road cycling. Summers can be very hot, especially in the open plains, so early mornings or late afternoons would be preferable during those months.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Granátula De Calatrava?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from around 18 reviews. Road cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from the open plains to the riverine landscapes, and the network of routes connecting historical towns.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or scenic spots along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views of the Campo de Calatrava plains and the Río Jabalón. For specific natural attractions, consider exploring areas near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3793255" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hervideros Baños del Barranco&lt;/a&gt; or the volcanic landscapes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1789437" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro Gordo Volcano&lt;/a&gt;, which can be found near some routes.

Q: What interesting landmarks can I explore near the road cycling routes?
A: The region is rich in history. Along or near the routes, you can find landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2895336" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almagro Main Square&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3854730" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of San Pedro (Almagro)&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2713622" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oreto and Zuqueca Archaeological Site&lt;/a&gt;, offering cultural stops during your ride.

Q: How long do road cycling routes typically take to complete?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and difficulty. For example, the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1251905687/pozuelo-de-calatrava-miguelturra-y-ermita-de-santa-marina-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pozuelo de Calatrava, Miguelturra y Ermita de Santa Marina — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt; takes about 3 hours 25 minutes, while the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1249647502/bolanos-torralba-y-carrion-de-calatrava-circular-desde-almagro" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bolaños, Torralba y Carrión de Calatrava — circular desde Almagro&lt;/a&gt; can be completed in about 1 hour 43 minutes.
tips: Granátula de Calatrava is a town located in the heart of Campo de Calatrava and is the heir to the ancient Roman Oretum and its subsequent Visigothic bishopric. It is an area where we can find volcanoes, quartzite hills and the Jabalón river valley.

This hermitage is believed to have been founded at the beginning of the 17th century, as a sign of the great faith and devotion of its citizens towards the Holy Trinity, demonstrated through wills, memory foundations and chaplaincies, always including the same phrase "in the name of the Most Holy Trinity, Father, Son and Holy Spirit, three persons, one true God".
Hermitage located on the road to Sta. Cruz de Mudela. It was run and assisted by the Trinitarian fathers, who in turn depended on the convent of the Holy Trinity in the town of Membrilla.
During the 19th century it was forgotten and left in a dilapidated state. Don Santiago Julio Maldonado y Maldonado-Cisneros, law graduate, natural and resident of the town, in his will when he died on December 10, 1886, ordered that the Hermitage of the Santísima Trinidad y San Antón be rebuilt at his expense, where the mortal remains of the testate, of his deceased parents (D. Santiago Maldonado Treviño and Dª María Antonia Maldonado Cisneros), of his wife (Dª Pilar Forcallo y Morales) and other successors in the board of trustees, ordered the construction of a building immediately next to the Hermitage of San Antón (Santísima Trinidad), which will have the shape and proportions of the plan, whose building would be used as a Hospital for the poor, and the family designated by their heirs must reside in it, in order to attend to its conservation and cleaning and whose family should be natural and neighbor of the Villa.

In the heart of the Sierra Morena region we find the very ancient municipality of Santa Cruz de Mudela, born from the medieval repopulations carried out by King Alfonso VIII after his victory against the Almohads in Las Navas de Tolosa. 76 km from its capital, it proudly shows its most beautiful buildings to locals and strangers, among which the church of the Assumption, the palace-house of Carlos Ortega and the hermitage of the Virtues, among others, stand out.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as 'moderate' due to its distance of over 90 km and an elevation gain of approximately 410 meters. While it features undulating roads with some flatter sections, it also includes climbs, particularly towards historic sites. Cyclists should have good to very good fitness to comfortably complete the full circular route, which typically takes around 3 hours and 45 minutes.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: The terrain primarily consists of undulating paved roads, characteristic of the Campo de Calatrava region. You'll encounter sections that are predominantly flat, interspersed with climbs, especially as you approach towns or historical points. The landscape is shaped by ancient volcanic activity, offering a unique backdrop as you cycle through extensive fields of cereals, vineyards, and olive groves. Expect quiet roads, often former main roads with reduced traffic.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect a rich blend of natural and historical scenery. The route takes you through the unique volcanic landscape of Campo de Calatrava, with views of agricultural fields, vineyards, and olive groves. You'll pass through towns like Granatula de Calatrava, Calzada de Calatrava, and Santa Cruz de Mudela, offering glimpses of traditional architecture and historical sites linked to the Order of Calatrava and the literary heritage of Don Quixote.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best times to undertake this road cycling trip are spring and autumn. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures, allowing you to avoid the intensely hot summers and potentially cold winters typical of the Castilla-La Mancha region. This ensures a more comfortable and enjoyable cycling experience.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. The Campo de Calatrava is a region, not a protected park with entry restrictions. You are free to enjoy the public roads and paths.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally welcome in the towns you pass through, this specific road cycling route is not ideal for dogs. The long distance (over 90 km) and the nature of road cycling on paved surfaces make it unsuitable for most dogs to accompany you safely and comfortably.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this circular route?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route, you can technically start from any of the towns mentioned, such as Calzada de Calatrava, Santa Cruz de Mudela, Villalba de Calatrava, or Granatula de Calatrava. You should be able to find public parking available in these towns, often near their respective town centers or main squares. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a road cycling route, sections of it briefly coincide with or cross other local paths. These include small portions of the Camino a Fontanar, Camino de Carnicabral, Cordel de Ganados, and Camino de las Cordilleras. However, the majority of the route follows established public roads.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or water along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several towns, including Calzada de Calatrava, Santa Cruz de Mudela, Villalba de Calatrava, and Granatula de Calatrava. These towns offer opportunities to refill water bottles, find cafes, restaurants, or small shops for refreshments and meals. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially between towns.

faqs.question: What are the historical highlights of the Campo de Calatrava region that this route passes through?
faqs.answer: The region is deeply rooted in history, particularly with the military and religious Order of Calatrava. Along this route, you'll encounter towns and sites that reflect this heritage. Notable highlights include the Hermitage of the Holy Trinity in Calzada de Calatrava and the Historic Center of Santa Cruz de Mudela. The entire area is dotted with castles and monuments that tell the story of its past.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, this is a circular road cycling route, meaning it starts and ends in the same general area, allowing you to return to your starting point without retracing your path. The route connects Calzada de Calatrava, Santa Cruz de Mudela, Villalba de Calatrava, and Granatula de Calatrava in a continuous loop.
